Group-level whole-brain task contrasts of joint action (leading and following) probe social interaction processes (role-taking, interpersonal coordination); meets global and social-processing criteria.
Analysis does not probe affiliation/attachment constructs or contrasts (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Analysis does not target social-communication tasks (e.g., face/gaze/emotion/auditory communication); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
Analysis does not implement canonical self-perception contrasts (e.g., Self>Other, self-judgment); missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

This conjunction ([Leading>Rest] ∩ [Following>Rest]) reflects shared sensorimotor network rather than explicit perception-of-others/mentalizing contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.
Group-level whole-brain conjunction in followers examines joint-action social processes; meets global and social-processing criteria.
No measures or contrasts of affiliation/attachment are present in this analysis (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Analysis does not specifically assay social-communication paradigms (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
No explicit self-related contrast or self-judgment task is present (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Conjunction map shows shared sensorimotor activations rather than explicit perception-of-others/mentalizing contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.
Whole-group Leading>Following contrast is a group-level whole-brain task contrast probing role-based social interaction and coordination; meets global and social-processing criteria.
Contrast does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Contrast is not a canonical social-communication task/contrast (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Leading>Following is not an explicit self-perception contrast (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).

The whole-group Leading>Following contrast and its reverse include activations in mentalizing/action-perception regions (TPJ, PCC, pSTS) associated with perception/understanding of others; meets global and perception-of-others criteria.
Leaders-only Leading>Following contrast is a within-group whole-brain task contrast probing role-based social coordination; meets global and social-processing criteria.
No affiliation/attachment measures or contrasts are present (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Task does not map onto canonical social-communication paradigms (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Although leaders engage self-oriented motor planning networks, the analysis lacks explicit self-perception contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Leaders-only analysis did not reveal the follower/mentalizing network and is not an explicit perception-of-others contrast (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).
Followers-only Leading>Following contrast probes social-interaction processes (role-specific following) at the group whole-brain level; meets global and social-processing criteria.
Analysis does not assess affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Although followers recruit social/perceptual regions, the paradigm is not a canonical social-communication task (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
No explicit self-related contrast (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).

Followers-only Leading>Following reveals follower-specific activations in TPJ, PCC, pSTS and related regions linked to perception/understanding of others; meets global and perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain regression of leader skill against Leading>Following is a task-evoked group-level analysis probing role-specific social processes; meets global and social-processing criteria.
Regression does not index affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Analysis does not target canonical social-communication contrasts (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Leader-skill regression focuses on motor/premotor regions and does not implement explicit self-perception contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Leader-skill regression does not highlight perception-of-others/mentalizing contrasts as its primary focus (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).

Group-level whole-brain deactivation map (regression with leader skill deactivations) is part of the task-evoked analyses probing social-role processes and meets global and social-processing criteria.
Deactivation map is not an affiliation/attachment task or contrast (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Deactivation analysis does not correspond to canonical social-communication paradigms (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Deactivation contrasts do not implement explicit self-processing contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Deactivation map is not primarily an explicit perception-of-others/mentalizing contrast (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).

Whole-brain regression of follower skill against Leading>Following probes role-specific social processes (mentalizing and action perception) at the group whole-brain level; meets global and social-processing criteria.
Regression with follower skill does not probe affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Although follower-skill regression implicates social-perceptual regions, it is not a canonical social-communication task/contrast (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Analysis targets perception-of-others/mentalizing regions rather than explicit self-related contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).

Follower-skill regression shows correlations in TPJ, PCC, pSTS, mPFC consistent with perception/understanding of others (mentalizing/action-perception); meets global and perception-of-others criteria.

The analysis is a group-level, whole-brain task contrast from a social/empathy task (Empathic Accuracy Task) in a healthy adult sample and measures social processing (emotional vs neutral videos).

The analysis does not measure affiliation or attachment constructs (no cooperation/kinship/loved-one vs stranger contrasts). Missing local inclusion criteria AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

The analysis derives from a task requiring reception of social communicative cues (video/audio of targets describing events) and the contrast examines processing of emotional communicative content, satisfying social communication criteria.
The analysis does not measure self-related perception or self-judgment (no Self > Other contrasts). Missing local inclusion criteria PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
The contrast examines empathic/emotional processing of others' states (emotional > neutral) and thus measures perception and understanding of others (empathy/ToM related).
Group-level whole-brain contrast from the Empathic Accuracy Task (neutral > emotional); produced from the same social task and thus qualifies as social processing evidence.
This contrast does not probe affiliation/attachment constructs (no cooperation/kinship/loved-one contrasts). Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Although the contrast is neutral > emotional, the data derive from reception of communicative video/audio stimuli and thus meet criteria for social communication (receptive non-facial communication).
The contrast does not index self-related processing (no Self > Other manipulation). Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Although showing regions more active for neutral clips, the contrast arises from a task designed to probe perception of others' emotions; thus it is included under perception of others as part of the task's social processing framework.
This whole-brain group-level modulation analysis tests relations between empathic accuracy (a social construct) and BOLD response, fitting social processing criteria.
Analysis of Z-EA modulation does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs. Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Modulation by empathic accuracy relates to processing of socially communicated emotional signals (receptive aspects), meeting social communication criteria.
This analysis does not assess self-related judgments or self > other contrasts. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
Z-EA modulation directly indexes neural correlates of accurately perceiving and understanding others' emotional states, satisfying perception of others criteria.
Negative modulation by Z-EA is still a whole-brain group analysis tied to a social/empathy task and thus meets social processing inclusion.
The negatively related regions to Z-EA do not reflect affiliation/attachment constructs. Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Regions negatively modulated by empathic accuracy are identified from a task requiring reception of communicative social cues, meeting social communication criteria.
This analysis does not test self-perception or self-monitoring contrasts. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
Negative modulation by Z-EA still concerns the neural substrates linked to understanding others' emotions (inverse relation), so it fits perception of others inclusion.
This whole-brain group-level correlation between BOLD and participants' ratings of target emotional intensity derives from a social/empathy task and measures social processing.
Correlation with emotional intensity ratings does not index affiliation/attachment constructs. Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The analysis captures participants' reception of communicated emotional intensity from targets over time (receptive social communication), satisfying social communication criteria.
This analysis does not probe self-related judgements or self > other contrasts. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
Correlation with participants' ratings of the targets' emotional intensity directly indexes perception and understanding of others' emotional states, meeting perception_others criteria.
Negative correlations between BOLD and emotional intensity ratings are from the same social task and thus qualify as social processing whole-brain results.
This analysis does not assess affiliation/attachment-related constructs. Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Even though correlations are negative, the analysis assesses reception of social communicative signals (ratings of target emotional intensity) and meets social communication criteria.
The analysis does not target self-related processes or contrasts. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Negative correlations are derived from participants' ratings of others' emotional intensity and therefore still index perception/understanding of others (inverse relation), meeting perception_others criteria.

Group-level whole-brain task (emotion perception from faces/vocals) in healthy adults measures social processing broadly (emotion recognition and social cue perception).
Task does not measure affiliation or attachment (no kinship, bonding, cooperation, or related contrasts). Missing inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1, AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The analysis tests reception of facial and vocal emotional signals (affective recognition), a core social-communication reception task performed at the group whole-brain level in healthy adults.
Task does not probe self-perception or self-judgment (no Self > Other or viewing self). Missing inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_SELF_I1, PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
The analysis measures perception/understanding of others' emotional states via facial and vocal expressions (group whole-brain), meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Group-level whole-brain task (emotion perception from faces/vocals) in healthy adults measures social processing broadly (emotion recognition and social cue perception).
Task does not measure affiliation or attachment (no kinship, bonding, cooperation, or related contrasts). Missing inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1, AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The analysis tests reception of facial and vocal emotional signals (affective recognition), a core social-communication reception task performed at the group whole-brain level in healthy adults.
Task does not probe self-perception or self-judgment (no Self > Other or viewing self). Missing inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_SELF_I1, PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
The analysis measures perception/understanding of others' emotional states via facial and vocal expressions (group whole-brain), meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Group-level whole-brain interaction analysis (Emotion × Modality) examines social-emotional processing (emotion perception across modalities) in healthy adults.
Interaction contrasts do not target affiliation/attachment processes (no kinship, bonding, cooperation contrasts). Missing inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1, AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The Emotion × Modality interaction directly addresses reception of facial and vocal emotional signals (social communication reception) at the whole-brain group level.
Interaction does not measure self-related perception or self-judgment. Missing inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_SELF_I1, PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
The interaction analysis assesses perception/understanding of others' emotions across modalities (faces vs voices), meeting perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain group map.

Global criteria are met: the NeuroSynth reverse-inference map is a group-level, whole-brain, task-evoked meta-analytic map derived from studies of social processing, and the independent dataset used healthy adult participants. The analysis explicitly targets 'social' studies and thus measures Social Processing.

Although global criteria are met, the analysis is a broad automated meta-analysis of studies containing the term 'social' and does not specifically measure Affiliation and Attachment. Missing required local inclusion criteria AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

Global criteria are met, but the analysis is a broad 'social' meta-analysis and is not specifically limited to Social Communication tasks/contrasts. Missing required local inclusion criteria SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
Global criteria are met, but the analysis does not specifically measure Perception/Understanding of Self. Missing required local inclusion criteria PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Global criteria are met, but the analysis is a broad meta-analysis of 'social' studies and is not specific to Perception/Understanding of Others (e.g., ToM/empathy contrasts). Missing required local inclusion criteria PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.

Contrast (Self vs. Celebrity) is a group-level whole-brain task-evoked map on healthy adults engaging social judgments (self/other), satisfying global criteria and measuring social processing.
Task does not specifically measure affiliation/attachment (no kinship/cooperation/attachment contrast); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Task does not assess social communication (faces, emotion, gaze, or communicative production); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
Self vs. Celebrity is a canonical self-judgment contrast in a whole-brain group map on healthy adults, meeting global and local criteria for Perception of Self.
Contrast is Self vs. Celebrity and does not measure perception/understanding of others (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).
Contrast (Friend vs. Celebrity) is a group-level whole-brain task-evoked map on healthy adults engaging social judgments about others, satisfying global criteria and measuring social processing.
Task does not explicitly index affiliation/attachment (no kinship/cooperation/attachment contrast); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Task does not assess social communication (faces, emotion, gaze, or communicative production); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
Contrast is Friend vs. Celebrity and does not index self-related perception (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Friend vs. Celebrity is a canonical other-referential contrast in a whole-brain group map on healthy adults, meeting global and local criteria for Perception/Understanding of Others.

This whole-brain regression identifies regions where the interaction (Creativity × Interdependence) modulates Self vs. Celebrity neural responses — a social-processing effect in a group-level task map, meeting global and local criteria.
Contrast examines moderation of creativity on self-referential activity, not affiliation/attachment per se; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Contrast does not measure social communication constructs (faces/emotion/gaze/etc.); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
This analysis specifically tests modulation of Self vs. Celebrity activity by interdependence and creativity in a whole-brain group map, satisfying global and Perception-of-Self local criteria.
Analysis targets self-referential modulation (Self vs. Celebrity) rather than perception/understanding of others; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.

This whole-brain regression identifies regions where the interaction (Creativity × Interdependence) modulates Friend vs. Celebrity neural responses — a social-processing effect in a group-level task map, meeting global and local criteria.

Although the contrast involves a close friend, the analysis assesses modulation of neural responses rather than direct affiliation/attachment measures; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Contrast does not index social communication processes (faces/emotion/gaze/etc.); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
Contrast is Friend vs. Celebrity focusing on others, not self-related perception; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
This analysis specifically tests modulation of Friend vs. Celebrity activity by interdependence and creativity in a whole-brain group map, satisfying global and Perception-of-Others local criteria.

Partnered vs non-partnered contrast taps cooperative, affiliative interaction (mutuality, social reward) consistent with affiliation/attachment constructs.
Partnering involves exchange of haptic cues (signalling and reception) and recruits areas for dynamic social communication (pSTS, S1/S2), meeting social-communication criteria.
Partnering contrast is primarily about social interaction rather than explicit self-related judgments or agency contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Partnering contrast recruits mentalizing and action-perception regions (TPJ, pSTS) tied to perception/understanding of others' actions and intentions.
Leading > conjunction isolates leader-specific social-role processing (self-oriented in joint action) within a social joint-action paradigm; meets social-processing criteria.
Leading-specific contrast targets motoric/self-initiation processes rather than affiliation/cooperative-bonding per se; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Leading involves signalling intentions to a partner (production side of haptic exchange); contrast examines leader-specific communicative aspects of joint action.
Leading shows self-orientation and self-initiated action (motor planning, agency-related regions); meets perception/self (agency) criteria.
Leading-specific contrast emphasizes self-initiation and motor planning rather than perceptual/mentalizing processes about others; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.
Following > conjunction identifies follower-specific social processes (heightened receptivity to partner cues, social motion perception) and recruits social-perception areas.
Following contrast focuses on sensory receptivity and motion-tracking rather than affiliation/attachment measures (cooperation/bonding); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Following captures the receptive side of haptic exchange (receiving partner cues) consistent with social-communication (reception) constructs.
Following is externally oriented (responsivity to partner cues) rather than testing self-judgment or agency; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Following activates regions for motion perception and social motion (pSTS, MT+/V5) and areas monitoring others' actions, meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Mutual > conjunction isolates symmetric, reciprocal interaction and engages mentalizing and social-reward areas (mPFC, TPJ, nucleus accumbens), consistent with social processing.
Mutual condition explicitly taps reciprocal cooperation and social reward (nucleus accumbens, amygdala), matching affiliation/attachment constructs.
Mutual interaction involves exchange and coordination (implicit signalling, mentalizing) and recruits communication-related networks (pSTS, TPJ, mPFC).
Mutual contrast emphasizes reciprocal social cognition and reward rather than explicit self-judgment/agency contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Mutual recruits mentalizing network (TPJ, mPFC, PCC) indicating representation and reasoning about others' intentions; meets perception-of-others criteria.
Improvisation contrast focuses on motor-planning and working-memory for improvised vs fixed actions rather than primarily testing social interaction; missing SOCIAL_PROCESSING_ALL_I1/I2.
Improvisation analysis is about generative motor processes and variability, not affiliation/attachment; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Improvisation contrast isolates internal generation of movement and motor networks rather than the exchange/reception of social signals; missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
Improvisation analysis addresses motor generation and working memory, not explicit self-judgment/agency contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Improvisation contrast does not primarily measure perception/understanding of others (e.g., theory of mind, empathy); missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.

Self-initiation contrast compares self-initiated versus externally-triggered actions in a paradigm that includes partnered conditions; it taps social-role aspects (self vs other-triggered) and thus meets social-processing criteria.
Self-initiation contrast targets agency and initiation processes rather than affiliation/attachment per se; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Self-initiation contrast is about internally- vs externally-triggered action (agency) rather than the exchange of social signals; missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
This contrast explicitly isolates self-initiated (agency-related) activity versus externally-triggered movement, meeting perception/self (agency) inclusion criteria.
Self-initiation contrast is focused on agency distinctions rather than testing perception/understanding of others' mental states; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.
Joint improvisation (Leading > Solo) directly examines partnered improvisation vs solo and addresses social interaction/signalling in joint action; meets social-processing criteria.
This contrast targets leader-specific signalling during improvisation rather than affiliation/cooperative-bonding measures; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Leading > Solo was designed to identify signalling of movement intentions to a partner (production of social signals), directly meeting social-communication criteria.
Joint improvisation contrast is focused on signalling to partner (interaction effect) rather than explicit self-judgment/agency contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.

Although joint improvisation involves a partner, this specific contrast probes leader signalling (production) rather than measuring representations of others' mental states or perception of others; missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.

Analysis 1a is a group-level, whole-brain contrast of observing social interactions (task involves social stimuli) in healthy adults and directly measures social processing.

The contrast measures perception/understanding of others by evaluating brain responses to observed social interactions (inferring intentions/affect) in a healthy adult sample using whole-brain group maps.

Cooperativity>affectivity indexes how participants perceive and understand others' shared action goals (intention understanding), meeting perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain group analysis of healthy adults.

This contrast isolates affectivity (affective contact) rather than affiliation/attachment per se (cooperation). Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

Affectivity>cooperativity targets affective social interactions (emotion-related cues, gaze, affect recognition), which fits social communication (receptive affect/face/gaze processing) in a whole-brain group map of healthy adults.
Contrast does not assess self-related judgments or self versus other processing. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
Affectivity>cooperativity reflects inference about others' affective/mental states (understanding others), satisfying perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain group analysis of healthy adults.

Global criteria met. The moral decision GLM examines choices about others (sister, friend, strangers) and engages mentalizing/empathy-related processes, satisfying perception/understanding of others criteria.

Contrast is a task-evoked whole-brain group contrast of Social-Reg > Social-Look, which directly probes socially-induced emotion regulation and thus measures social processing.

The primary contrast does not specifically measure affiliation/attachment (e.g., loved one vs stranger). Although attachment scores were correlated in separate analyses, the contrast itself does not operationalize affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
The contrast does not probe social communication constructs such as face/gaze or explicit communicative reception/production (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
The contrast is not designed to measure self-perception or self-judgment (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).

The Social-Reg > Social-Look contrast recruits canonical mentalizing/social-cognitive regions (dmPFC, TPJ, precuneus) implicated in perception/understanding of others (the contrast measures processes consistent with theory-of-mind/other-understanding).
The conjunction includes the Social-Reg condition and identifies common task-evoked, whole-brain activations across Social- and Self-Reg, thus indexing social processing as part of the analysis.
This conjunction contrast identifies shared regulation-related regions and does not specifically measure affiliation or attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
The conjunction does not target social communication subconstructs (faces, gaze, expressive behaviors) and thus does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
The conjunction identifies common regulation-related control regions but does not specifically probe self-judgment or self > other contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
The conjunction captures general cognitive-control regions common to both tasks rather than regions selectively measuring perception/understanding of others (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).
This analysis isolates areas specifically recruited by Social-Reg (task-evoked whole-brain contrast) and therefore indexes social processing related to socially-induced regulation.
Although these distinct Social-Reg areas overlap with social-DMN, the contrast itself does not directly operationalize affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
The analysis identifies mentalizing/social-cognitive regions but does not target social communication tasks (faces, gaze, etc.), so SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2 is not met.
The distinct Social-Reg areas index understanding others/mentalizing rather than self-judgment or self-perception (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).

This analysis specifically highlights dmPFC, precuneus, and left TPJ—regions classically implicated in perception/understanding of others (the contrast directly measures mentalizing/other-understanding).
This analysis isolates areas specifically recruited by Self-Reg (self-induced reappraisal) rather than a social task; it does not meet SOCIAL_PROCESSING_ALL_I1/I2.
The Self-Reg-specific contrast does not measure affiliation or attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2).
Self-Reg distinct areas are not derived from a social communication task (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2).
Although Self-Reg concerns self-directed regulation, the contrast does not specifically operationalize self-perception or self-judgment contrasts (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
The Self-Reg-specific contrast does not index perception/understanding of others or mentalizing (missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2).

Global criteria met (healthy adult sample, group-level whole-brain task-evoked contrasts). The task explicitly manipulates social reputation and subjective pleasantness, so this contrast measures social processing.

Global criteria are met but the task does not specifically measure affiliation or attachment (e.g., kinship, close relationships, cooperation). Missing local inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Global criteria met. Task involves viewing faces paired with verbal comments and rating affective response—this captures reception of facial/verbal social communication.
Global criteria met. The task asks subjects to imagine comments directed at themselves and rate pleasantness (self-related evaluation), meeting Perception/Self criteria.

Global criteria are met but the contrast targets self-directed reputation processing rather than assessing others' states or mentalizing about others. Missing local inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.
Global criteria met (healthy adult sample, group-level whole-brain task-evoked contrasts). The contrast isolates neural responses to negative social reputation, which is clearly social processing.

Global criteria are met but the task does not specifically measure affiliation or attachment constructs (e.g., kinship, close-relationship contrasts). Missing local inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Global criteria met. The negative vs neutral contrast involves reception of face-plus-verbal comments and thus measures social communication/reception.
Global criteria met. The contrast reflects participants' self-referential evaluation of negative comments directed at themselves (self-perception).

Global criteria are met but the analysis focuses on how subjects perceive comments about themselves rather than representing others' mental states or traits. Missing local inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.
Global criteria met. This inclusive-mask identifies regions responding to social reputation (both positive and negative), so it measures social processing.

Although global criteria are met, the conjunction contrast does not specifically index affiliation/attachment processes or relationships; it captures general reputation processing. Missing local inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Global criteria met. The conjunction of positive and negative > neutral involves processing of face+verbal comments—reception of social communication.
Global criteria met. The conjunction captures regions engaged when subjects process comments directed at themselves (self-related perception/evaluation).

Global criteria are met but the contrast indexes self-directed reputation processing rather than inferring others' mental states or traits. Missing local inclusion criteria: PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.
Global criteria met. This contrast isolates regions specifically associated with positive social reputation processing (vs negative), directly measuring social processing.
Global criteria are met but the contrast contrasts valence of reputation rather than affiliation/attachment constructs; does not meet AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Global criteria met. The contrast compares social verbal/face stimuli of differing valence and thus probes social communication processing (reception).
Global criteria met. The contrast reflects self-referential valuation of positive vs negative comments directed at the subject (self perception/valuation).
Global criteria are met but this analysis tests valence of comments about the self rather than representing others' states/traits—does not satisfy PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.
Global criteria met. This contrast isolates regions specifically associated with negative reputation processing relative to positive, which is social processing.
Global criteria are met but the contrast does not index affiliation/attachment constructs (e.g., close relationships); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Global criteria met. The contrast involves reception of face+verbal comments (negative vs positive) and thus measures social communication reception.
Global criteria met. The contrast captures participants' self-related responses to negative vs positive comments (self-perception/valuation).
Global criteria are met but the analysis focuses on self-directed reputation processing rather than inferring others' mental states or traits. Missing PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 and PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I2.

The analysis is a whole-brain group-level contrast from a healthy adult sample examining gaze/eye-contact effects—clearly a social processing task measuring processing of social cues.
This contrast does not target affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
The contrast involves viewing faces and eye-gaze (direct/averted) and gaze-following, which are canonical social communication measures.
The task does not probe self-related processes (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Analyses probe perception and interpretation of others' gaze and attentional relations, fitting perception-of-others constructs.
Whole-brain group-level healthy adult contrast examining social gaze (faces looking away vs. eye contact)—measures social processing.
This contrast does not target affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Contrast contrasts face gaze conditions (direct/averted) relevant to social communication (reception/production of gaze cues).
No self-related judgment or self-perception is assessed in this contrast (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Faces’ gaze and inferred attentional relations concern perception/understanding of others, meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain contrast (congruent > incongruent) on gaze-following in healthy adults, clearly assessing social processing.
Contrast addresses gaze congruency/attention rather than affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Gaze congruency and gaze-following are core social-communication phenomena (eye-gaze reception/production).
No measures of self-perception or self-judgment are present (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Contrasts reflect how participants perceive and interpret others’ gaze and attentional relations, satisfying perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain group contrast (incongruent > congruent) examines social gaze-induced attention shifts in healthy adults—meets social processing criteria.
This contrast focuses on gaze congruency/attention not affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
Incongruent vs congruent gaze cues involve processing of social communicative signals (gaze), meeting social communication criteria.
No self-related processing is assessed in this contrast (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
This contrast indexes processing of others’ gaze and inferred attentional relations, satisfying perception-of-others inclusion criteria.
Whole-brain contrast (EC congruent > AG congruent) assesses how observed eye contact modulates gaze processing—fits social processing construct.
Contrast tests modulation of gaze by observed eye contact, not explicit affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
This interaction contrast directly probes communicative aspects of gaze (ostensive signals, eye contact) and thus meets social communication criteria.
No self-referential processing is targeted by this contrast (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Contrast concerns perception/interpretation of others’ eye contact and gaze relations, aligning with perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain contrast (AG incongruent > AG congruent) examines gaze congruency effects under averted-gaze context—social processing measure.
The contrast addresses gaze congruency and attention, not affiliation/attachment (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
This contrast involves perception of gaze cues (congruent vs incongruent) in a social context, meeting social communication inclusion criteria.
No self-related judgments are involved in this analysis (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Contrast probes how participants perceive and interpret others' incongruent vs congruent gaze in an averted context, fitting perception-of-others criteria.

Group-level whole-brain contrast of a social-affective mimicry task that probes social processing (faces, group membership, mimicry). Meets global and local criteria.
This contrast is a general mimicry regulation contrast (Incongruent>Congruent) not specifically targeting affiliation/attachment; does not meet AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Contrast involves hand-mimicking and face stimuli (viewing faces/imitative behavior) and is a whole-brain task contrast, satisfying social communication criteria.
Incongruent>Congruent captures self-other distinction / agency-related control in imitation-inhibition tasks (rTPJ, control processes); meets perception-self criteria.
Contrast involves perception of others' actions and social cues (action perception, empathy-related regions); satisfies perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain group contrast isolating mimicry regulation specifically for happy (affiliative) faces; directly probes social processing.
Contrast isolates mimicry regulation for happy (affiliative) faces and is directly tied to reciprocation of affiliation; meets affiliation/attachment criteria.
Task involves viewing facial expressions and imitative responses; this contrast measures social communication-related processes.
Happy-specific mimicry regulation engaged rTPJ and self–other distinction processes; fits perception-self (agency/self-other) inclusion criteria.
Contrast reflects processing of others' affiliative signals (faces) and their actions; meets perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain contrast isolating mimicry regulation for angry faces; examines social-affective processing of facial cues and mimicry.
This contrast targets angry (non-affiliative) expressions and does not measure affiliation/attachment per se; does not meet AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Contrast involves viewing emotional faces and their influence on imitation; satisfies social communication criteria.
Angry-specific mimicry contrast does not target self-related judgments or agency distinctions explicitly and did not show robust self-related activations (does not meet PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2).
Contrast concerns perception of others' emotional expressions (anger) and their action cues; meets perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain contrast isolating mimicry regulation for out-group faces; directly addresses social processing of group membership and mimicry.
Out-group mimicry was interpreted as an affiliative/appeasement response; this contrast measures affiliation-related modulation of mimicry and meets affiliation criteria.
Contrast involves viewing faces that signal group membership and their effect on imitative behavior; satisfies social communication criteria.
Out-group specific contrast emphasizes perception of others and salience/threat-related regulation (AI/MCC) rather than self-focused processes; does not meet PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Contrast specifically probes perception/representation of out-group others (threat salience, group cues) and action perception; meets perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain contrast isolating mimicry regulation for in-group faces; probes social processing related to group membership and imitation.
In-group-specific mimicry modulation taps affiliation-related processes (ingroup affiliation/reciprocation); meets AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Contrast involves processing in-group faces and imitative responses; meets social communication criteria.
In-group specific contrast does not primarily index self-related judgments or agency processes and thus does not meet PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Contrast probes perception of in-group others and action-related processing (imitation), satisfying perception-of-others criteria.

Global criteria met (healthy adult sample, group-level whole-brain task contrast). The why>how task includes social stimulus categories (emotional expressions and intentional actions) and the contrast isolates attributional (social) processing across stimulus types, satisfying the local social processing criteria.

Although the study investigates social attribution, it does not target affiliation/attachment processes (e.g., kinship, cooperation, loved-one vs stranger). Missing local inclusion criteria AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

The analysis is a domain-general why>how conjunction across social and nonsocial stimuli and does not specifically isolate social communication (e.g., reception/production of facial or communicative signals). Missing local inclusion criteria SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
The task and contrast do not measure self-related processing or self-judgment. Missing local inclusion criteria PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Global criteria met. The why>how contrast includes mental-state attributions about people (emotional expressions and intentional actions) and therefore measures perception and understanding of others (theory-of-mind/mentalizing processes).
Global criteria met. This contrast tests stronger why>how responses for social versus nonsocial stimuli, directly indexing social processing and attributional reasoning about people.

The contrast isolates general social attributional processing (mental-state inference) but does not target affiliation/attachment constructs such as kinship, cooperation, or loved vs stranger comparisons. Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

Global criteria met. The contrast specifically identifies regions responding more to attributional (why) processing for social stimuli (emotional expressions and intentional actions) than nonsocial stimuli; this includes reception of facial/emotional cues and related social-communication processes.

This analysis contrasts social versus nonsocial attributional processing about others and does not measure self-related judgments or self-perception. Missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Global criteria met. The contrast isolates brain responses selectively elevated for social (emotion and action) attributional processing versus nonsocial, directly indexing understanding of others' mental states (theory of mind/mentalizing).

Group-level whole-brain contrasts of deception vs. truth in an interactive sender–receiver game directly probe social processing (the intention to deceive, ToM, social interaction).
Although interpersonal, the task probes strategic deception and ToM rather than affiliation/attachment or bonding; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
The paradigm involves explicit message exchange (production of non-facial social communication) and contrasts (deception vs. truth) index communicative behavior.
The contrasts do not probe self-judgment or self-focused processing; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.
The analyses explicitly target understanding others' mental states (intention to deceive, expecting receiver beliefs) and report rTPJ/ToM-related activations.
Group-level whole-brain contrast of simple deception vs. truth probes social cognitive processes (deception, intention, ToM).
Contrast targets strategic deception/ToM rather than affiliation or attachment; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
Simple deception involves sending false messages (production of social communication); the contrast measures communicative behavior.
No self-judgment/self-focused task contrasts are present; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.
Simple deception requires inferring the receiver's beliefs (ToM) and elicits rTPJ activation, satisfying perception-of-others criteria.
Sophisticated deception vs. truth contrasts probe social cognition (intention to deceive, belief about receiver) at the group whole-brain level.
The analysis examines strategic deception/ToM rather than affiliation or bonding; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
Sophisticated deception involves communicative intent and message production (social communication); the contrast measures this communicative behavior.
The contrast does not target self-related judgments or self-awareness; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.
Sophisticated deception explicitly requires representing and predicting others' beliefs (ToM); the contrast and reported rTPJ activations meet perception-of-others criteria.
The contrast comparing sophisticated vs. simple deception isolates social-cognitive differences (ToM demands) in a whole-brain group analysis, meeting social processing criteria.
This contrast contrasts two forms of deception (ToM demands) not affiliation/attachment; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
Both conditions involve communicative acts; the contrast captures differences in communicative/social information processing (production/inference), fitting social communication criteria.
Contrast compares forms of deception and does not probe self-judgment or self-related processing; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.
Sophisticated vs. simple deception contrast directly addresses demands on representing others' mental states (ToM) and shows bilateral TPJ/temporal activations, meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Truth vs. deception contrasts probe social processing (decision to tell the truth under social incentives) and identify habenula/opercular activations in a whole-brain group analysis.
The contrast isolates genuine truth-telling vs. deception (reward/control) rather than affiliation/attachment; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
Truth vs. deception involves communicative acts—the content and intention of messages—so it meets social communication inclusion criteria.
Although involving intention, the contrast does not index explicit self-judgment or self-referential evaluation tasks; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.
Genuine truth vs. deception contrasts concern expectations about the receiver's belief and involve social inference processes (ToM), satisfying perception-of-others criteria.
The parametric whole-brain analysis models incentive to deceive in an interactive social task, indexing social processing related to deception and conflict.
This parametric contrast indexes conflict/incentive for deception rather than affiliation or attachment; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2 are not met.
Parametric modulation of incentive-to-deceive relates to communicative decision-making (message sending) and thus meets social communication criteria.
The parametric model addresses conflict/incentive for deception, not explicit self-related judgment or self-awareness; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 are not met.

Modeling incentive to deceive still depends on anticipating the receiver's choice and beliefs (ToM); reported dACC/amPFC modulation occurs in the social decision context, meeting perception-of-others criteria.

This analysis does not measure self-related judgments or self vs other comparisons; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Contrast probes perception/understanding of others' communicative intent (gesture comprehension, feeling addressed) and links to mentalizing regions; meets perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain healthy group contrast.
Group-level whole-brain contrast (ego>allo) in healthy adults probing social orientation effects on feeling-addressed judgments and social cue processing.
Contrast does not assess affiliation/attachment constructs (no measures of bonding/cooperation); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Actor orientation (egocentric>allocentric) modulates communicative intent and social signaling; this is a whole-brain task contrast in healthy adults relevant to social communication.
Contrast does not target self-related judgments or self>other comparisons; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Egocentric vs allocentric orientation reflects perception/understanding of others' communicative intent and engages mentalizing networks; meets perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain healthy group contrast.
Group-level whole-brain contrast (allo>ego) in healthy adults probing social orientation effects and processing of social cues (allocentric>egocentric).
Contrast does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs (no cooperation, kinship, or bonding tasks); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

Allocentric>egocentric orientation contrast addresses social communicative context and processing of communicative cues; whole-brain group analysis in healthy adults meets social communication criteria.
This contrast does not target self-awareness or self-related judgments; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Allocentric orientation modulates perception/understanding of others' communicative stance (reduced addressment), engaging theory-of-mind and perception-of-others processes; meets local and global criteria.

Interaction contrast ((IC-ego>IC-allo)>(NG-ego>NG-allo)) is a whole-brain group-level test in healthy adults probing how social cues (gesture x orientation) affect feeling-addressed judgments — clearly social processing.
Interaction does not measure affiliation/attachment (no bonding/cooperation task); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Interaction probes the combined effects of co-speech gesture and body orientation on perceived addressment — a core social communication question; whole-brain group contrast in healthy adults.
Interaction contrast does not operationalize self-judgment or self>other comparisons; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Interaction examines how others' gestures and orientation influence perceived addressment and engages mentalizing/understanding-others processes (ACC correlation), meeting perception-of-others criteria.

Interaction contrast ((IC-allo>NG-allo)>(IC-ego>NG-ego)) is a whole-brain group-level test assessing how gesture effects depend on orientation for feeling-addressed judgments — clearly within social processing.
This analysis does not assess affiliation/attachment constructs (no bonding/cooperation measures); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Contrast tests gesture-by-orientation effects on communicative perception (IC vs NG across ego/allo), meeting social communication local criteria in a whole-brain healthy group contrast.
Analysis does not probe self-related judgments or self>other contrasts; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.

Interaction examines how gestures influence perceived addressment in different orientations, engaging understanding of others' intentions and correlating with mentalizing regions (SMA/IFG/Insula correlations), fulfilling perception-of-others criteria.

The analysis is a whole-brain group-level contrast in healthy adults probing joint attention in an interactive social task; it directly measures social processing (responding to joint attention).

The task and contrast probe joint attention/communication rather than measures of affiliation or attachment (e.g., cooperation/relationship quality). Missing required inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The contrast isolates responding to social gaze (joint attention) and thus targets social communication processes (gaze following/eye-contact).

The task requires representation and monitoring of one's own attentional state/agency during joint attention; the whole-brain contrast likely captures self-related processing (self-monitoring/agency).
Responding to joint attention requires representing another's attentional focus and intentions (gaze following); the contrast targets perception/understanding of others' attention.
Whole-brain group contrast in healthy adults isolating initiating joint attention; clearly measures social processing related to interactive social behavior.

The initiating joint attention contrast targets social-communicative and attentional processes rather than affiliation/attachment per se. Missing required inclusion criteria: AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
Initiating joint attention involves production of communicative gaze and evaluating partner responses; the contrast maps social communication processes.
Initiating trials require self-monitoring, representing one's own attentional state and agency when guiding the partner; the contrast likely captures self-related processing.
IJA requires representing the partner's attentional state (to detect readiness and verify joint attention); the contrast measures perception/understanding of others.
The conjunction identifies regions common to initiating and responding joint attention — a core social processing result from whole-brain group contrasts in healthy adults.
The conjunction reflects joint attention neural overlap, not measures of affiliation/attachment; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The conjunction highlights regions engaged for production and reception of communicative gaze (shared network for gaze-based interaction), meeting social communication criteria.
The conjunction was interpreted by authors as reflecting parallel representation of self- and other-oriented attention; thus it indexes self-related perceptual/monitoring processes.
The conjunction identifies regions (e.g., TPJ, pSTS) involved in representing others' attention/intentions — directly meeting perception-of-others criteria.
This whole-brain contrast isolates additional activation for initiating vs responding in a social interactive task, reflecting social processing differences.

The contrast targets differential neural recruitment for initiating vs responding joint attention, not affiliation/attachment measures; missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The contrast captures differences in communicative gaze production/evaluation between initiating and responding roles, meeting social communication criteria.
The IJA>RJA contrast reveals regions implicated in self-monitoring/agency and increased demand on representing one's own attention when initiating, satisfying perception-self criteria.
This contrast shows increased recruitment of regions (e.g., TPJ, IFG, precuneus) tied to representing others' attention in initiating vs responding, meeting perception-of-others criteria.

Group-level whole-brain univariate contrast (friend > computer) from a healthy adult sample on a social communication task; directly measures social processing related to perception and interpretation of social signals (sender identity).
Contrast compares messages from a real-life friend versus a computer (friend > PC), which reflects affiliation/attachment-related processing (loved one vs non-social/computer).

The analysis decodes communicative content (affective text messages) during a communication task (receptive social communication), meeting social_communication criteria.
The MVPA contrasts message content and does not index self-related judgments or self-representation (missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 / PERCEPTION_SELF_I2).
Decoding of affective message content (happy vs sad) reflects perception/understanding of others' emotional states (messages describe others), satisfying perception_others criteria.

Analysis is a whole-brain group-level conjunction of triad, self, and other conditions from a healthy adult sample and directly assesses social processing constructs (triadic interaction, self- and other-referential processing).

The conjunction reflects common mentalizing activations across triad, self, and other conditions but does not specifically measure affiliation or attachment constructs (e.g., cooperation/competition, kinship, loved-one vs stranger). Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.
The analysis is not from a task targeting social communication per se (faces, gaze, emotion recognition, mimicry). Missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.

Although the conjunction includes the self condition, it is not a contrast specifically measuring self-referential processing (e.g., Self > Other); thus it does not meet PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2 for a self-focused analysis.

Although the conjunction includes the other condition, the analysis is not a targeted contrast of other-referential processing (e.g., Other > Other baseline) and therefore does not meet PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2.
Triad > (Self & Other) is a whole-brain group contrast in healthy adults that isolates triadic social interaction processing and thus indexes social processing.

The triad contrast isolates triadic interaction-related activations but does not specifically target affiliation/attachment constructs (e.g., cooperation vs competition, loved-one vs stranger). Missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2.

The triad contrast concerns imagined triadic interactions and mirror/mentalizing recruitment but is not a social-communication task (faces, gaze, explicit communicative signals). Missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 and SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I2.
This contrast isolates triadic > (self & other) activations and does not specifically measure self-referential processing (PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2 not met).

Although the triad condition involves another agent, the contrast identifies triad-specific activations (not an Other > baseline contrast) and therefore does not meet PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1/I2 for an other-focused analysis.
Self > (Other & Triad) is a whole-brain group contrast in healthy adults assessing self-referential processing within a social task context and thus indexes social processing.
The self contrast targets self-referential processing and does not assess affiliation/attachment constructs (missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 and AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I2).
The Self > (Other & Triad) contrast is not a social-communication task (faces, gaze, mimicry) and does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.

Interns rated intentions to recommend (a communicative intent) during encoding; the contrast indexes processes that underlie social communication (production of communicative intent).

The intention effect produced activations in MPFC and precuneus/posterior cingulate, regions associated with self-relevance and self-related processing, indicating the contrast measures perception/understanding of self.

The salesperson effect indexes how well interns shape others' preferences (persuasion/communication), mapping onto social communication (production of persuasive communication and its consequences).

The salesperson effect was exclusively associated with bilateral TPJ (mentalizing) rather than self-related midline regions; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 for self-related processing.
The salesperson effect was associated with bilateral TPJ, a core mentalizing/theory-of-mind region, directly measuring perception/understanding of others.

The buzz effect reflects successful transmission of messages across individuals (exchange of socially relevant information), fitting social communication constructs.

The buzz effect implicated ventral striatum and mentalizing regions (TPJ, DMPFC) rather than core self-related midline processing; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2 for self-related processing.

Whole-brain group contrasts of partner 'yes' vs 'no' in a speed-dating task clearly index social processing (perception of social signals, responses to social outcomes).
Contrast compares receipt of romantic interest (yes) versus rejection (no), directly indexing affiliation/attachment-related processes (formation/receipt of social bonds).

Contrast indexes social outcome/reward (partner decision) rather than primary measures of social communication (e.g., faces > non-faces or explicit facial expression/communication reception); does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
Contrast concerns perception of partner decisions and social reward, not explicit self-judgment or self-referential processing; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Receiving another's decision (yes/no) targets representation of others' states/intentions (observing partner's liking), meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Whole-brain group contrast of 'no' > 'yes' (rejection/disinterest) indexes social processing of negative social feedback.
Contrast examines social rejection/disinterest outcomes, which directly relate to affiliation/attachment processes (negative social bond signals).

Contrast reflects social outcome valence (rejection) rather than primary communicative signal processing (no direct Faces > non-faces or facial-expression reception contrast); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
Contrast focuses on others' decisions and their affective impact rather than explicit self-evaluation or self-referential judgment; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Rejection/disinterest outcomes reflect attribution or representation of others' choices/intentions, meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Match > Unrequited contrasts mutual romantic interest versus non-mutual outcomes, clearly indexing social processing of affiliation and reward.
Match (mutual yes) versus Unrequited directly contrasts outcomes related to forming romantic/affiliative bonds (affiliation/attachment).
Contrast compares social outcomes (mutuality) and reward value rather than primary social-communication signal processing (does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2).

Although self-reported desirability relates to this contrast, the Match > Unrequited contrast is not an explicit self-judgment or self-vs-other comparison per PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2; missing those inclusion criteria.
Contrast involves interpreting others' reciprocal interest (mutual vs non-mutual), indexing representation of others' social intentions/states.
Rejection > Disinterest isolates neural responses to social rejection (no from desired partner) and thus indexes social processing.
Rejection versus Disinterest contrasts outcomes directly tied to affiliation/attachment processes (negative outcome for a desired affiliative partner).
Contrast targets affective impact of rejection rather than explicit communicative signal reception/production (does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2).
This contrast indexes response to others' rejection and affective processing rather than explicit self-evaluation or self-focused judgments; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Rejection implies representation of another's negative choice; this engages processes for understanding others' decisions/intentions.
Mismatched > matched contrasts unexpected social outcomes and updating, a core social-processing operation.
Mismatched outcomes (e.g., rejection, unrequited) directly concern affiliative success/failure and thus relate to affiliation/attachment.
Contrast emphasizes expectation violations and belief-updating about partners, not primary reception/production of communicative signals (missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2).
Mismatched > matched focuses on updating beliefs about others and expectation violations rather than explicit self-evaluation or self-referential tasks; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
This contrast explicitly indexes updating representations of others' intentions/choices (pSTS/RMPFC), meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Unsigned prediction errors from an RL model about partner decisions index social-learning and processing of social information.
Prediction errors concern learning about one's desirability to others and violations in affiliative feedback—relevant to affiliation/attachment.
The analysis indexes surprise/learning signals about partner decisions rather than direct communicative signal reception/production; does not meet SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
Unsigned prediction errors here reflect learning about one's own desirability/self-image (participants' expectation about how desirable they are), satisfying self-perception inclusion criteria.
Prediction errors are computed from observed partner decisions and relate to modeling others' choices; this meets perception-of-others criteria.
Contrast at face appearance for partners given a 'yes' vs 'no' indexes anticipatory social processing and valuation of social partners.
Anticipation for partners to whom the participant said 'yes' reflects affiliative motivation and attention to potential social bonds, meeting affiliation criteria.

Although faces are shown, this contrast isolates anticipatory valuation for chosen partners rather than core social-communication measures (e.g., faces > non-faces or explicit emotion/communication reception); missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
Contrast indexes anticipation toward others rather than explicit self-focused judgments or tasks; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Viewing partners' faces with context of the participant's own choice engages representation/anticipation of others' potential responses and intentions, meeting perception-of-others criteria.
Contrast at face appearance for partners given 'no' vs 'yes' indexes social processing of anticipated non-affiliative partners and negative social value.
Anticipation of partners to whom the participant said 'no' relates to affiliative judgments and avoidance of social bonds, relevant to attachment/affiliation processes.
Contrast isolates anticipatory valuation/processing for unchosen partners rather than primary communicative signal processing; missing SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1/I2.
This anticipatory contrast concerns others rather than explicit self-evaluation/self-judgment tasks; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Anticipatory responses to others' faces in the context of social choice engage representations of others' potential actions/intentions, meeting perception-of-others criteria.

Contrast does not specifically measure affiliation or attachment (e.g., loved one vs stranger); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Viewing famous faces involves receptive facial communication processes (faces > baseline) in a whole-brain group contrast on healthy adults; meets global and social communication criteria.
Analysis does not measure self-related processing (no self-judgment or viewing of self); missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Famous faces contrasts probe representations and knowledge about other people (person memory/identification); meets global and perception-of-others criteria.
Personally familiar (friends) faces vs baseline is a whole-brain group contrast in healthy adults specifically measuring social/person-related processing.
Contrast involves personally familiar friends and a social closeness task (close friends vs unknown), directly measuring affiliation/attachment.
Viewing and judging personally familiar faces engages receptive facial communication processes; whole-brain group contrast meets social communication criteria.
Task does not involve self-related judgments or viewing the self; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Personally familiar faces require retrieval of information about others (names, biographical knowledge, social closeness); meets perception-of-others criteria.
The conjunction of famous and familiar faces reflects shared face/person knowledge processing across whole-brain group contrasts in healthy adults and indexes social processing.

Overlap of face-related activations (famous ∩ familiar) arises from face viewing/person-recognition contrasts and therefore indexes receptive facial communication processes.
No self-related processing is measured by the conjunction map; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Conjunction reflects shared activations for recognizing and retrieving information about other people (familiar/famous), satisfying perception-of-others criteria.
Faces > Landmarks is a whole-brain group contrast on healthy adults contrasting face stimuli (social) vs landmarks; measures social processing.

Faces > Landmarks directly tests receptive facial processing (faces vs non-faces) in a whole-brain group-level contrast; meets social communication criteria.
No self-related task or contrast (no self>other); missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1/I2.
Faces > Landmarks involves perception/recognition of other people (faces) and thus satisfies perception-of-others criteria in a whole-brain group analysis.

Famous faces > Novel faces examines person-knowledge/familiarity effects in a whole-brain group contrast on healthy adults and indexes social processing.
Contrast compares famous to novel faces but does not specifically probe affiliation/attachment (e.g., loved one vs stranger); missing AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1/I2.
Contrast involves face stimuli and examines face-related information (familiarity), fitting receptive facial communication criteria in a whole-brain group analysis.

MC answer ToM>PI recruits STS, temporal poles and language areas; task involves processing facial/auditory social information and language—meets social communication criteria.
Contrast targets mental state reasoning about others (not self-related judgments); PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.
MC ToM>PI is a within-group ToM contrast directly measuring understanding of others' mental states; satisfies global and local perception_others criteria.

T-PICA IC spatial maps derive from a model-free multivariate decomposition (ICA/T-PICA) and are not univariate group-level task-evoked voxelwise statistical contrast maps as required by GLOBAL_I2; thus excluded by GLOBAL_E2.
Excluded because the analysis is a task-related ICA component (T-PICA) not a group-level univariate whole-brain task-evoked statistical map (GLOBAL_E2 applies).

Although component maps relate to face/language networks, the analysis method (T-PICA) is a multivariate ICA decomposition and does not meet the required univariate whole-brain task-evoked map criterion (GLOBAL_E2).
Analysis excluded due to use of T-PICA (ICA-based multivariate decomposition) rather than a group-level univariate whole-brain statistical map (GLOBAL_E2).
Though IC1 is related to social perception, the T-PICA component is not a univariate group-level task contrast (GLOBAL_E2 applies), so GLOBAL inclusion criteria are not satisfied.
IC2 is from T-PICA (multivariate ICA) and thus not a univariate group-level task-evoked voxelwise contrast; excluded under GLOBAL_E2.
Excluded because analysis uses T-PICA (ICA-based decomposition) rather than a whole-brain univariate task contrast (GLOBAL_E2).
Although component includes STS/IFG, the analysis method (T-PICA) is a multivariate component decomposition and not a group-level univariate task map required by GLOBAL_I2; excluded (GLOBAL_E2).
Excluded due to T-PICA multivariate ICA method; does not meet global whole-brain univariate map requirement (GLOBAL_E2).
IC2 relates to processing of others but is derived from T-PICA (not a univariate group-level task contrast); fails GLOBAL inclusion and is excluded by GLOBAL_E2.
IC3 results from T-PICA (ICA-based), not a univariate group-level task-evoked voxelwise contrast; therefore excluded by GLOBAL_E2.
Excluded because this analysis is an ICA/T-PICA component map rather than a whole-brain univariate task contrast measuring affiliation/attachment (GLOBAL_E2).
Although IC3 implicates dmPFC and IFG, the method is T-PICA (multivariate ICA) and not a univariate group-level statistical map as required by GLOBAL_I2; excluded (GLOBAL_E2).
While IC3 is discussed as self-referential (dmPFC), the analysis is an ICA component and not a univariate group-level task contrast; excluded by GLOBAL_E2.
IC3 is task-related but derived from T-PICA (ICA), not a group-level univariate voxelwise contrast; fails GLOBAL inclusion and is excluded by GLOBAL_E2.

Does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs (no task contrasts like loved one vs stranger or cooperation/competition); AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Contrast does not target social communication (faces/gaze/explicit communicative signals); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Contrast indexes perception of others' pain rather than self-referential processing or agency/self-judgment; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.
Contrast measures perception/understanding of others' painful states (empathy for pain); meets global and perception-of-others criteria.
The No pain > pain contrast does not specifically index social processing (it highlights non-painful > painful stimuli) and therefore does not meet SOCIAL_PROCESSING_ALL_I2.
Does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Does not target social communication (faces/gaze/explicit communicative signals); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Contrast does not measure self-referential processing or agency; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.

Whole-brain group contrast directly manipulates social context (presence of another individual) and indexes social processing; meets global and local criteria.
Contrast manipulates presence of another but does not measure affiliation/attachment (no bonding/cooperation comparisons); AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Not a social-communication task (no faces/gaze/explicit communicative signals); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Contrast contrasts presence vs absence of another person in stimuli but does not index self-referential processing or agency judgments about the subject; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.
Contrast indexes processing related to presence of other agents (theory-of-mind/social perception); meets global and perception-of-others criteria.
Group-level contrast manipulating social context (greater activation when only one person present) is part of the social-context manipulation and thus meets social processing criteria.
Does not target affiliation/attachment constructs (no bonding/cooperation contrasts); AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Not a communication-specific contrast (no faces/gaze/expressive exchange); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Contrast labels 'self' refer to stimulus composition (one person present) rather than participant self-reflection or agency judgments; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.
This contrast highlights activity when others are absent (Self > Self+Other) and does not index perception/understanding of others; PERCEPTION_OTHERS_I1 not met.

Correlation with emotional contagion does not measure affiliation/attachment (no bonding/cooperation contrasts); AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Analysis does not index social-communication processes (faces/gaze/explicit communicative signals); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Correlation concerns empathic responding to others' pain rather than self-reflection or agency; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.

Correlation identifies regions whose responses to observed pain covary with individual differences in emotional contagion—directly relevant to perception/understanding of others; meets global and local criteria.

Pain-rating correlations do not assess affiliation/attachment constructs; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.

Correlation of pain ratings with neural responses during observed pain directly indexes perception/understanding of others' pain (empathy); meets global and local criteria.
Whole-brain correlation between emotional contagion trait and responses during PCO/observed pain caused by others indexes social-emotional processing; meets global and local criteria.
Correlation with emotional contagion during PCO does not measure affiliation/attachment constructs; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Not targeting social-communication processes (faces/gaze/explicit communicative signals); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.

Pain-rating correlations during PCO do not assess affiliation/attachment constructs; AFFILIATION_ATTACHMENT_I1 not met.
Not focused on social-communication (faces/gaze/expressive exchange); SOCIAL_COMMUNICATION_I1 not met.
Correlation with pain ratings pertains to perception of others' pain rather than self-referential processing; PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 not met.
Correlation of PCO pain ratings with neural responses directly indexes perception/understanding of others' pain in a social-agency context; meets global and local criteria.

The contrast does not involve self-related judgments or viewing the self; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
Contrast measures perception/understanding of others' emotional states (masked sad faces), meeting perception-of-others criteria at the group whole-brain level.
Group-level whole-brain contrast (masked happy > neutral) in healthy adults measuring perception of social emotional faces; meets global and local social processing criteria.

The contrast does not involve self-related judgments or viewing the self; missing PERCEPTION_SELF_I1 and PERCEPTION_SELF_I2.
